title: Gulf Economies Face Deeper Contractions as Yen Tests 40-Year Lows, ECB Hawks Eye September Hike
subhead: A Reuters poll shows most Gulf economies contracting harder than expected as Hormuz disruptions make it impossible to sell oil even at higher prices, while Japan's finance minister signals decisive yen intervention and ECB officials push for a September rate hike as European gas prices surge.

sections.heading: Gulf Economies Slump Harder Than Expected as Hormuz Blocks Oil Exports
sections.body:
A Reuters poll of economists conducted July 7-16 found that most Gulf Cooperation Council economies will contract more sharply this year than expected just three months ago, with the damage coming not from low oil prices but from the inability to ship oil at all.

Kuwait and Qatar suffered the steepest forecast downgrades, both now expected to slump 8.1% this year — compared with April projections of 4.4% and 6.0% contractions respectively. Bahrain is forecast to shrink 5.1%, up from 2.9%. The UAE has shifted from stagnation to a 0.5% contraction. Only Saudi Arabia and Oman are still expected to grow, thanks to pipeline and port alternatives that bypass Hormuz.

Saudi growth was cut to 1.4% from 2.6%, below the IMF's 1.7% projection, as the kingdom's East-West pipeline to the Red Sea provides only partial relief. Oman's estimate was raised to 3.1% from 2.2%, since its export terminal sits outside the strait.

Marwan Barakat, group chief economist at Bank Audi, cautioned: 'The issue is not only the level of oil prices or interest rates, it is the physical ability to move hydrocarbons, goods and people through one of the world's most important chokepoints.' Fitch Solutions senior analyst Abdalla Saleh noted the 2027 rebound forecasts assume 'tensions with Iran ease and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz gradually returns to normal over the next six to 12 months.'

Akanksha Samdani, lead economist at Oxford Economics, flagged the structural risk: 'The biggest risk isn't necessarily another major military escalation. It's the possibility that businesses permanently price in a higher geopolitical risk premium.' Gulf inflation remains contained — Saudi 2.1%, UAE 2.9%, Qatar 3.2% — thanks to dollar pegs, subsidies, and fiscal buffers absorbing cost pass-through.
sections.whyItMatters: The Gulf forecast revision reveals the core paradox of the Hormuz crisis: higher oil prices do not compensate producers who cannot physically export. Countries with alternative routes (Saudi, Oman) still grow; those without (Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain) face deep contractions. The 2027 rebound projections depend entirely on a de-escalation that shows no signs of materializing.

sections.heading: Japan's Katayama Warns of Decisive Yen Action as GPIF Shift Looms
sections.body:
Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama used her strongest language in weeks to warn of possible currency intervention, stating that Tokyo is prepared to take 'decisive action' on the yen if needed. The dollar stood at 162.39 yen on Thursday, near the multi-decade high of 162.84 hit earlier this month, with the yen hovering around its lowest level in nearly 40 years.

Katayama also opened the door to a potentially far larger structural shift, saying the government wants a 'substantial' increase in domestic asset investment by the Government Pension Investment Fund — the world's largest pension fund with roughly $1.5 trillion in assets. Analysts cited by Bloomberg said GPIF has the greatest capacity among Japanese investors to influence the forex market, as it conducts a strategy review every five years and can adjust holdings within its target allocation bands.

The combined signal — verbal intervention plus the GPIF possibility — represents a shift in Japan's approach. Previous yen intervention operations involved direct dollar-selling estimated at tens of billions of dollars, with limited lasting impact. A GPIF reallocation toward domestic bonds could reduce structural outflows that have weakened the yen for years.

Separately, Bloomberg's top currency forecaster projected the yen could reach 170 against the dollar, adding pressure on Japanese authorities to act before the currency's decline becomes self-reinforcing through import price inflation.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is running out of conventional intervention options. Verbal warnings and spot-market dollar sales have not stemmed the yen's decline. Katayama's GPIF signal suggests Tokyo may be preparing a structural response — redirecting Japan's largest institutional investor toward domestic assets — that could have lasting effects on global bond flows and the dollar-yen equilibrium.

sections.heading: Dollar Set for Weekly Loss as Fed Rate Bets Recede, but Energy Risks Linger
sections.body:
The US dollar index rose 0.31% to 100.76 on Thursday but was still on track for a weekly decline, after softer-than-expected inflation data reduced the case for an immediate Fed rate hike.

Market pricing via CME Group Fed funds futures shows just a 10% probability of a July rate hike, down from 45% at the start of the week, and a 48% chance of at least a 25bps increase by September. The shift reflects June CPI falling 0.4% month-on-month — the largest monthly drop in four years — and producer prices declining 0.3%, the biggest wholesale drop in 14 months.

However, the dollar found support from safe-haven flows as US-Iran tensions escalated. Mesirow Currency Management senior strategist Uto Shinohara noted: 'With seasonal and World Cup-related distortions clouding today's data, and geopolitical developments continuing to have a relatively restrained market impact, gains in the dollar, Treasury yields and oil have remained relatively measured so far.'

Bannockburn Capital Markets chief strategist Marc Chandler observed: 'Maybe the dollar is finding a safe haven bid. Iran threat has been a longstanding fear since the war began. The US escalation has some limits and Iran is demonstrating it.'

US retail sales rose 0.2% in June, down from 1% in May, reflecting lower gasoline prices during the brief ceasefire period. Gasoline spending fell 5.3%, while sales at all other retailers climbed 0.7%.
sections.whyItMatters: The dollar's weekly decline reflects a market that believes June's soft inflation data gives the Fed breathing room. But the 10% July hike probability understates the two-sided risk: energy prices have surged since the ceasefire collapsed, and the July and August CPI prints will capture that reversal. The next six weeks of data will determine whether September becomes a live meeting or a non-event.

sections.heading: ECB Hawks Circle as European Gas Prices Signal September Rate Hike
sections.body:
The European Central Bank is expected to hold interest rates at its July 23 meeting, but a Reuters poll found economists increasingly anticipate a rate hike by September as European gas prices surge to their highest levels since March.

European benchmark gas rose 0.9% to 55.03 euros per megawatt-hour on Thursday, compounding concerns that the Iran conflict's energy supply disruption will feed through to eurozone inflation. The gas price spike is particularly significant because Europe imports a far larger share of its energy through Middle East channels than the US, making the ECB more exposed to the conflict's price effects.

ING global macro head Carsten Brzeski said some ECB officials 'might actually be inclined to push more forcefully for another rate hike' given the Middle East escalation. Markets are currently pricing two additional ECB rate hikes into 2027, and some economists have not ruled out a move as early as next week.

The euro fell 0.23% to $1.1437, ending a two-day winning streak. FX Empire analysis noted that the key risk for euro bulls is that a prolonged energy shock shifts market focus away from higher interest rate differentials and toward the growth-damaging effects of imported energy inflation — a dynamic that could weaken the euro even as the ECB tightens.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB faces a different calculus than the Fed: Europe is far more exposed to Middle East energy disruption, and gas prices are already sending that signal. If September brings both a Fed hike and an ECB hike, the synchronized tightening would compound the global monetary squeeze. But if European gas prices keep rising, the ECB may be forced into a hike that damages growth — a stagflationary trap.

sections.heading: Oil Steadies Near $85 as Markets Price Prolonged Hormuz Disruption
sections.body:
Brent crude was little changed around $85 per barrel on Thursday, with WTI at $80. Oil has gained for four consecutive sessions but moves have been moderate — a sign that markets are pricing in a prolonged but not catastrophic Hormuz closure rather than an acute supply shock.

Capital Economics chief economist Neil Shearing noted: 'It feels to us that markets are still expecting that the disruption to flows will prove short-lived, but the perceived probability of a more extreme outcome has increased.' He warned that if the strait remains closed for an extended period, oil markets could reach a tipping point, triggering a price spike to $120 or higher.

The relative calm in prices has been supported by the departure of oil-laden tankers that had been stranded in the Strait of Hormuz for months, providing some near-term supply relief. But Export Development Canada forecasts oil will average $96 per barrel this year and nearly $84 in 2027, reflecting ongoing uncertainty. EDC chief economist Stuart Bergman said storage tanks 'have become the oil market's marginal producer,' with inventories below seasonal norms after crisis-era draws.

Gold futures fell 0.1% to $4,007.50, briefly dipping below $4,000 on energy price concerns. Forex.com analyst Fawad Razaqzada said: 'Persistently high energy prices would make it difficult for the Fed to adopt a more dovish stance. That is one reason we're seeing the U.S. dollar regain a bit of momentum.'

Middle East sovereign credit default swap spreads rose, with Bahrain's five-year CDS climbing 6bps to 285bps and Qatar's rising 1bp to 33bps, per S&P Global Market Intelligence.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil at $85 with four days of moderate gains is not a panic price — it is a market pricing in a slow-burn disruption. The real risk is inventory depletion: once strategic stocks drawn down during the crisis can no longer buffer supply, the market could gap sharply higher. The CDS spreads on Gulf sovereign debt confirm that credit markets are already pricing prolonged distress in the region.

sections.heading: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Escalates Trade War Risk With India and China
sections.body:
The bipartisan Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026 proposes tariffs of up to 100% on countries that continue importing Russian crude oil, specifically targeting India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan. The bill, initially championed by the late Senator Lindsey Graham and backed by President Trump, has 26 bipartisan Senate co-sponsors.

The revised legislation reduced the maximum penalty from an earlier 500% tariff proposal to 100%, and exempts approximately 15 European countries and Japan that import less than 15% of Russia's natural gas exports or are taking steps to reduce dependence on Moscow.

Indian officials reacted with sharp criticism, accusing Washington of 'double standards.' A senior official told The Economic Times: 'Making India a punching bag for US foreign policy failures will be counterproductive.' India imports over 88% of its crude oil from overseas markets and has increased Russian crude purchases since 2022.

The bill includes a presidential waiver authority allowing the White House to suspend tariffs on individual countries if enforcement is deemed harmful to US national security or global economic stability. Energy experts have warned that removing large volumes of Russian crude from global markets could trigger a sharp price increase, creating risks for consumers worldwide.

The bill's passage could also face legal challenges following recent Supreme Court decisions restricting the executive branch's ability to impose broad trade measures without clear congressional authority.
sections.whyItMatters: This bill would weaponize tariffs against third-party countries in an unprecedented way — not sanctioning Russia directly, but penalizing its customers. India and China are the world's two largest incremental oil buyers, and removing their access to Russian crude would tighten an already strained market. The European exemption creates a credibility problem that New Delhi is already exploiting diplomatically. The presidential waiver gives the White House an escape valve, but the legislative signal itself is already reshaping trade expectations.

bottomLine: The Reuters Gulf poll crystallizes the central paradox of the Hormuz crisis: oil prices are elevated but not spiking, because the market assumes the disruption is temporary — even as every data point says otherwise. Kuwait and Qatar face 8% contractions; only Saudi Arabia and Oman can still export via alternative routes. Meanwhile, Japan's Katayama is preparing a two-track yen defense (verbal intervention plus a possible GPIF restructuring), the ECB is eyeing a September hike as European gas prices surge, and the dollar is caught between soft US inflation data and hard energy-driven inflation expectations. The Senate Russia sanctions bill adds a second trade-war front, targeting India and China with 100% tariffs that would further tighten oil markets if enacted. The common thread: every major central bank and every major trade policy is now being shaped by the same unresolved variable — whether the Hormuz closure is temporary or permanent.
sources: Reuters (via AOL, Hiru News, CNA), Bloomberg, Morningstar/Dow Jones, Firstpost, Cyprus-Mail, Rigzone, UPI, Outlook Business, Jakarta Post, Capital Economics, Fitch Solutions, Oxford Economics, Bank Audi, S&P Global Market Intelligence, Export Development Canada, CME Group, ING, Bannockburn Capital Markets, Mesirow Currency Management, Forex.com, FX Empire
